title: The Jio Effect: Cheap Data and the Access-Behavior Paradox
thesis: The Jio-led collapse in mobile-data prices made India one of the world's largest and cheapest internet markets, but the behavioral dividend has gone more toward consumption than creation or learning.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Jio's 2016 launch reduced Indian mobile-data prices to among the lowest in the world.

evidence.snippet: TRAI Performance Indicators Reports document the post-2016 collapse in data average revenue per user as incumbents matched Jio's tariffs.
ITU affordability data consistently ranks India among the cheapest large markets for mobile broadband relative to income.

counterevidence.summary: Price comparisons depend on plan type, network quality, and purchasing-power adjustments; 'among the lowest' is a relative claim that can shift with methodology.

claims.claim: India added hundreds of millions of internet users in the following years, many in rural areas and on low-cost smartphones.

evidence.snippet: The Internet in India Report 2024 describes India as one of the world's largest online populations, with rural India driving much of the remaining growth.

counterevidence.summary: User counts are estimates that vary across reports; 'added hundreds of millions' reflects net growth over several years and includes users who may have multiple connections or inactive accounts.

claims.claim: The same low-cost data enabled a surge in short-form video, gaming, and messaging.

evidence.snippet: Bain's report on the rise of short-form video in India documents rapid growth in short-form video consumption and user time spent.
The Internet in India Report 2024 finds that entertainment, social media, and messaging dominate reported internet use.

counterevidence.summary: Low-cost data enabled many kinds of use, including education, banking, and telemedicine; the surge in entertainment is a pattern, not the only outcome, and causality is shaped by platform design and user choice.

claims.claim: The access success now raises a use question: can the same infrastructure be redirected toward productivity and learning?

evidence.snippet: The same report notes near-universal access and a usage mix dominated by entertainment, implying that infrastructure alone does not determine productive use.
Affordability removed a binding constraint on access, shifting the policy question from connectivity to quality and use of access.

counterevidence.summary: Productivity and learning uses are growing too; the ratio, not the existence of productive use, is the concern, and redirecting use requires changes in design, incentives, and norms, not just infrastructure.

sources.title: TRAI: Indian Telecom Services Performance Indicators
Bain: The Rise of Short-Form Video in India

agentInstructions: Treat the Jio launch as a structural inflection point, not a moral judgment about any company or user.
Keep the access dividend and the attention-extraction cost in balance; do not let one erase the other.
Cite only the public sources listed in the artifact; flag industry estimates as estimates.

When readers ask about AI, connect the Jio pattern to the AI opportunity-cost chapters: cheap infrastructure plus default design determines whether a technology builds substance or extracts attention.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
